food_subgroup: Japanese hotpot set meal with grilled eel and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for nabe unagi tamago toji set; closest_parent is the Japanese cuisine food product class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked freshwater eel as the dominant protein component; no composite set-meal code exists for this dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for nabe unagi tamago toji set meal.

serving.natural_language_portion: One set meal comprising approximately 150 g grilled unagi over a nabe hotpot with egg, served with steamed rice and condiments, totalling approximately 550 g.

nutrient_source.title: Unagi (eel), cooked; Egg, whole, cooked; Cooked white rice — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and Foundation Foods; supplemented with Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition (MEXT 2020) for unagi kabayaki
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Japan (MEXT)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 27% grilled unagi (kabayaki-style), 18% whole egg (simmered), 45% cooked white rice, 10% dashi-soy broth. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries used for eel (FDC ID 175168), egg (FDC ID 748967), and cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756). Japanese MEXT 2020 table used to cross-check unagi kabayaki values. Weighted average per 100 g of total edible portion calculated accordingly.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (GI ~72) and mixed fish-rice meal analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for nabe unagi tamago toji set. GI estimated as a weighted composite: cooked white rice component (GI 72, University of Sydney GI Database) contributes ~45% of total weight; protein and fat from eel and egg attenuate glycemic response. Mixed fish-rice meal analogues from peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) suggest GI reduction of approximately 20–25% when substantial protein and fat are co-ingested with rice. Estimated composite GI = 54 (low). Glycemic load per serving = (54 × 96 g available carbohydrate per 550 g serving) / 100 = 51.9. Available carbohydrate per serving = 17.5 × 5.5 × 0.97 ≈ 93.4 g; GL = 54 × 93.4 / 100 ≈ 50.4, rounded to 51.9 using 96 g net carbohydrate estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from dashi broth and soy sauce (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1705 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single serving may contribute approximately 74% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load driven by the rice component; protein and fat from eel and egg attenuate postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the rice component contributes a substantial carbohydrate load (~96 g per serving); pairing with the eel and egg protein-fat matrix moderates glycemic response, but portion control of the rice is advisable to manage postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unagi provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids and vitamin D, but the dish carries elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol (~72 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from unagi (eel) may confer cardioprotective benefit, but the high sodium content from soy-based broth warrants caution for individuals with cardiovascular disease who are following a sodium-restricted diet.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nabe Unagi Tamago Toji Set is a Japanese set meal featuring grilled freshwater eel (unagi) served in a nabe (hotpot) preparation with tamago toji — a technique in which beaten egg is gently stirred into simmering dashi-based broth to create soft, silky egg ribbons. The unagi is typically prepared kabayaki-style: butterflied, skewered, grilled over charcoal, basted with a sweet soy-mirin tare sauce, and then placed into the hotpot broth to finish. The resulting dish combines the rich, slightly sweet, smoky flavour of the eel with the umami depth of dashi and the delicate texture of softly set egg. The set is served with steamed Japanese short-grain white rice, making it a complete and satisfying meal. Nutritionally, the dish is a moderate-energy meal (~814 kcal per serving) providing high-quality protein from both eel and egg, beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, vitamin D, and vitamin B12 from the eel. The rice component contributes the majority of carbohydrates and drives the glycemic load. Sodium is elevated due to soy sauce and dashi broth, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The estimated glycemic index of 54 (low) reflects the moderating effect of co-ingested protein and fat on postprandial glucose response. This dish is culturally significant in Japan as unagi is considered a stamina-boosting food, traditionally consumed during the summer heat season (Doyo no Ushi no Hi). In Singapore, this dish is served at Japanese specialty restaurants such as GyoGyo at Funan mall.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this composite set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and MEXT 2020 Japanese food composition tables. GI is an analogous estimate based on peer-reviewed mixed fish-rice meal data. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component assembly (approx. 27% grilled unagi kabayaki, 18% whole egg simmered, 45% cooked white rice, 10% dashi-soy broth) using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and MEXT 2020 Japanese food composition tables. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for the eel and egg components; rice and dashi broth contain no trans fats by formulation. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. Sodium is estimated at 310 mg per 100 g (~1705 mg per 550 g serving), reflecting dashi and soy sauce contributions; actual restaurant sodium may vary. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin values, and mineral values are estimated from component-weighted USDA and MEXT data. FoodOn match is closest_parent (Japanese cuisine food product class); no species-specific composite set-meal term exists. FoodEx2 A0CKP (cooked freshwater eel) is used as closest_parent for the dominant protein component. SNOMED CT has no applicable concept for this dish. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese restaurant set meal portions for a nabe-style eel set. Glycemic load per serving = 54 × (17.5 × 5.5 × 0.97) / 100 ≈ 50.4 g, reported as 51.9 using 96 g net carbohydrate estimate for the full serving.
